section.title: God Brings Down and Lifts Up
section.sectionRationale: A brief but weighty hymn of Asaph, this psalm answers the previous lament's cry for God to act with God's own declaration that He alone judges, appointing the time to bring down the proud and lift up the humble, a truth Mary would later sing in the Magnificat.
section.thesis: Because God alone holds the appointed time of judgment in His hand, exalting one and humbling another, the proud are warned not to boast while the righteous are called to thanksgiving and confident, ongoing praise.
section.frameNote: Four movements: thanksgiving for God's nearness, verse 1; God's appointed judgment and warning to the proud, verses 2 to 5; God alone exalts and judges, verses 6 to 8; ongoing praise and the final reversal, verses 9 to 10.

verses.text: For the choirmaster: To the tune of "Do Not Destroy." A Psalm of Asaph. A song. We give thanks to You, O God; we give thanks, for Your Name is near. The people declare Your wondrous works.
"When I choose a time, I will judge fairly.
When the earth and all its dwellers quake, it is I who bear up its pillars. Selah
I say to the proud, 'Do not boast,' and to the wicked, 'Do not lift up your {{qeren|horn}}.
Do not lift up your horn against heaven or speak with an outstretched neck.'"
For exaltation comes neither from east nor west, nor out of the desert,
but it is God who judges; He brings down one and exalts another.
For a {{kos|cup}} is in the hand of the LORD, full of foaming wine mixed with spices. He pours from His cup, and all the wicked of the earth drink it down to the dregs.
But I will proclaim Him forever; I will sing praise to the God of Jacob.
"All the horns of the wicked I will cut off, but the horns of the righteous will be exalted."

qeren.gloss: Horn, a symbol of strength, authority, and pride, as in an animal displaying its horns. To 'lift up the horn' pictures boastful self-exaltation; God alone decides whose horn is cut off and whose is lifted.

kos.gloss: Cup, often used in the prophets as an image of divine judgment poured out and drunk down, as here, or of blessing, depending on context; here it pictures the inescapable judgment the wicked must drink to the dregs.

basic.context: A short hymn of Asaph, likely answering the desperate plea of Psalm 74 for God to rise up, this psalm gives voice to God's own declaration that He alone appoints the time of judgment, bringing down the proud and lifting up the humble.
basic.hermeneutics: This psalm proclaims a timeless truth about God's sovereign judgment; it is not describing one isolated historical event but declaring the pattern by which God governs all human pride and humility, past, present, and future.
basic.originalLanguages: The proud are warned not to lift up their qeren, horn, a symbol of boastful strength, verses 4-5, while God's judgment is pictured as a kos, cup, of foaming wine that the wicked must drink to the dregs, verse 8.
basic.typology: The cup of God's judgment that the wicked must drink anticipates the cup of wrath Jesus drank on behalf of sinners in Gethsemane and at the cross, so that those who trust Him are spared what this psalm says the wicked cannot escape.
basic.christ: Christ prayed, 'Let this cup pass from me,' yet drank the very cup of divine judgment pictured in verse 8, Matthew 26:39, so that all who trust Him never have to drink it themselves, while His own subsequent exaltation, His horn lifted up, fulfills the psalm's promise that God exalts the righteous, Luke 1:69.
basic.god: God alone is shown to hold the timing and outcome of all judgment, unmoved by any human claim to power from any direction, bringing down the proud and lifting up the humble according to His own perfect timing.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 26:39 shows Jesus facing the cup of God's wrath this psalm pictures. Luke 1:52 has Mary sing that God 'has brought down rulers... and lifted up the humble,' directly echoing this psalm's theme. Luke 1:69 speaks of God raising up 'a horn of salvation,' the positive fulfillment of this psalm's horn imagery.
basic.oneStory: This declaration that God alone brings down the proud and lifts up the humble threads through Mary's Magnificat into the whole story of Scripture, resolved at the cross where Christ drank the cup of judgment pictured here so that all who trust Him are lifted up instead of cast down.

context.literary: A hymn built around alternating voices, the congregation's thanksgiving, verse 1, God's own first-person speech, verses 2-5 and 10, and the psalmist's reflection and vow of praise, verses 6-9, unified by the recurring image of the horn as a symbol of strength.
context.historical: Attributed to Asaph and set to the tune 'Do Not Destroy,' shared with several psalms associated with deliverance from national threat, this psalm likely arose from a moment when Israel needed reassurance that God, not any human power, controls the rise and fall of nations.
context.canonical: Following directly after Psalm 74's desperate lament over the ruined sanctuary, this psalm functions as God's own answer, declaring that He has already appointed the time and manner of judgment on the arrogant enemy.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To declare, on God's own authority, that exaltation and humiliation come from Him alone, warning the proud not to boast and assuring the righteous that God will judge fairly in His appointed time.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes God's sovereign pattern of governing pride and humility across history; it prescribes humility before God and confident, ongoing praise rather than either despair or self-exaltation.

debates.question: Does the 'cup' of verse 8 picture a single historical judgment on a specific nation, or God's judgment in general on all the wicked of every era?

views.label: A specific historical judgment tied to the psalm's original occasion
views.case: The tune 'Do Not Destroy' links this psalm to a cluster of psalms tied to particular deliverances, suggesting the cup imagery originally addressed one identifiable threat to Israel.

views.label: A general and repeated pattern of divine judgment on all the wicked of the earth
views.case: The phrase 'all the wicked of the earth' in verse 8 is universal in scope, and the imagery recurs elsewhere in Scripture, Isaiah 51:17, Revelation 14:10, describing God's judgment in every age, not one isolated event.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that whatever specific deliverance first prompted this psalm, its declared principle, that God alone judges and exalts, applies as an unchanging pattern across every era, culminating in the final judgment.

study.originalLanguages: The proud are warned against lifting up their qeren, horn, a picture of boastful strength, verses 4-5, while God's judgment on the wicked is pictured as a kos, cup, of foaming wine they must drink to the dregs, verse 8.

typology.type: The cup of judgment drunk to the dregs, verse 8
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the cup of God's wrath Jesus drank at Gethsemane and the cross on behalf of sinners, Matthew 26:39.

typology.type: Horns cut off, horns exalted, verse 10
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the humbling of the proud and the raising up of 'a horn of salvation' in Christ, Luke 1:69.

study.christ: The cup of foaming wine the wicked must drink to the dregs, verse 8, anticipates the cup of divine wrath Jesus prayed might pass from Him yet willingly drank at Gethsemane and the cross, Matthew 26:39, so that those who trust Him are spared the judgment this psalm describes. His own resurrection and exaltation fulfill the psalm's promise that God lifts up the horn of the righteous, echoed in Zechariah's prophecy of 'a horn of salvation... in the house of His servant David,' Luke 1:69.
study.god: God alone appoints the time of judgment and determines who is exalted and who is brought low, unmoved by any claim to power from any direction on earth, holding the whole earth steady even as He judges it.

crossReferences.note: Jesus faces the very cup of divine judgment this psalm pictures, praying it might pass yet submitting to drink it.
Mary's Magnificat directly echoes this psalm's theme, that God brings down rulers and lifts up the humble.
Zechariah's prophecy of a 'horn of salvation' fulfills this psalm's promise that the horns of the righteous will be exalted.

study.oneStory: This short hymn's declaration that God alone brings down the proud and lifts up the humble threads through Mary's song into the whole story of Scripture, resolved at the cross where Christ drank the cup of judgment pictured here so that all who trust Him are exalted rather than condemned.

quiz.q: What does the congregation give thanks for in verse 1?

options.text: That God's Name is near and His wondrous works are declared among the people.
options.note: The psalm opens with thanksgiving rooted in God's nearness and known deeds.

options.text: A recent military victory described in detail.
options.note: No specific battle is described; the thanksgiving centers on God's nearness and works generally.

options.text: A new king's coronation ceremony.
options.note: No coronation is mentioned; the focus is thanksgiving for God's own nearness.

options.text: The rebuilding of a destroyed city.
options.note: No such rebuilding is described in this psalm.

quiz.q: What warning does God give to the proud and wicked, verses 4-5?

options.text: Not to boast or lift up their horn against heaven.
options.note: This is God's direct warning against arrogant self-exaltation.

options.text: To increase their wealth as quickly as possible.
options.note: The opposite; the warning is against boastful pride, not a call to gain wealth.

options.text: To build higher walls for protection.
options.note: No such instruction appears; the warning concerns pride, not fortifications.

options.text: To seek alliances with foreign nations.
options.note: The psalm addresses pride before God, not foreign policy.

quiz.q: According to verses 6-7, where does exaltation come from?

options.text: Neither from east, west, nor the desert, but from God alone, who judges.
options.note: This directly states that no direction or human source grants true exaltation, only God.

options.text: From military conquest alone.
options.note: The text explicitly denies that any earthly direction or power source grants exaltation.

options.text: From inherited royal bloodlines only.
options.note: The psalm attributes exaltation to God's judgment, not merely bloodline.

options.text: From the size of one's army.
options.note: The text denies any human source as the origin of true exaltation.

quiz.q: What does the cup in God's hand represent, verse 8?

options.text: Judgment that the wicked of the earth must drink down to the dregs.
options.note: This vivid image pictures inescapable divine judgment on the wicked.

options.text: A reward given only to the faithful.
options.note: The cup here specifically represents judgment on the wicked, not reward for the faithful.

options.text: A covenant meal shared among all nations.
options.note: The image is one of judgment poured out, not a shared covenant meal.

options.text: A blessing of abundant wine for a festival.
options.note: The context makes clear this cup is judgment, not festival blessing.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's theme connect most directly to the New Testament?

options.text: Mary's Magnificat echoes it directly, declaring God brings down rulers and lifts up the humble, Luke 1:52.
options.note: This is a clear and direct New Testament echo of the psalm's central theme.

options.text: It predicts the exact date of a future battle.
options.note: The psalm makes no such specific prediction.

options.text: It has no clear connection to the New Testament.
options.note: Its theme is directly echoed in Luke 1:52 and its cup imagery in Matthew 26:39.

options.text: It teaches that all wealth is inherently evil.
options.note: The psalm addresses pride and God's sovereign judgment, not wealth itself as inherently evil.

questions: The psalm opens with thanksgiving because God's Name is near, verse 1. What specific evidence of God's nearness can you give thanks for today?
God warns the proud not to lift up their horn or boast, verses 4-5. Where in your own life are you tempted toward that same self-exalting boastfulness?
The psalm insists that exaltation comes from God alone, not from any human direction or effort, verses 6-7. Where are you tempted to seek promotion or significance apart from God?
Jesus drank the very cup of judgment pictured in verse 8 so that His people would not have to. How does remembering what that cup cost Him change the way you receive God's mercy today?

The psalmist resolves to keep proclaiming God forever regardless of present circumstances, verse 9. What would it look like for you to keep praising God even while waiting for Him to set something right?

translationNotes.note: 'Do Not Destroy' is the name of a tune to which this psalm was set, shared with Psalms 57, 58, and 59, likely referencing a specific occasion of deliverance from destruction.
'I will proclaim Him' can also be rendered 'I will proclaim it,' referring either to God Himself or to His works; either reading fits the psalm's call to ongoing, forever praise.

No em or en dashes present. No sermon from the trusted cluster could be confirmed on Psalm 75 this session, so status is 'none' rather than risk fabrication.
